[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"). The [2008 Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Maldives "Constitution of Maldives") or "*Fehi Gānoon*" declares the significance of [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") in the country. The constitution requires that citizenship status be based on adherence to the state religion, which legally makes the country's citizens 100% Muslim.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Yousuftabrizi.jpg/250px-Yousuftabrizi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yousuftabrizi.jpg)

A Plaque in [Hukuru Mosque](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9_Friday_Mosque "Malé Friday Mosque"), Malé, Maldives, placed by [Sultan Ibrahim Iskandhar](/wiki/Ibrahim_Iskandar_I "Ibrahim Iskandar I") on which Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari's name is written; his last name can also be read as "at-Tabrizi" instead of "al-Barbari"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Friday_mosque_minaret_Male1981.jpg/250px-Friday_mosque_minaret_Male1981.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friday_mosque_minaret_Male1981.jpg)

[Malé Friday Mosque](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9_Friday_Mosque "Malé Friday Mosque") Minaret, 1981

Islamic influence in the Maldives may date as far back as the 10th century, with mentions of the region by Arabic accounts dating to around the 9th and 10th centuries.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Some Maldivians were already Muslim before the conversion of the king around 1153.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The importance of the [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") as traders in the Indian Ocean by the 12th century may partly explain why the last [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") king of Maldives [Dhovemi](/wiki/Dhovemi_of_the_Maldives "Dhovemi of the Maldives") converted to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") in the year 1153[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) (or 1193, for certain copper plate grants give a later date).[[4]](#cite_note-:23-4) The king thereupon adopted the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") title and name (in Arabic) of [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") (besides the old Dhivehi title of *Maha Radun* or *Ras Kilege* or *Rasgefānu*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]) Muhammad al-Adil, initiating a series of six Islamic dynasties consisting of eighty-four [sultans](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") and [sultanas](/wiki/Sultana_(title) "Sultana (title)") that lasted until 1932 when the sultanate became elective.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) The formal title of the Sultan up to 1965 was, *Sultan of Land and Sea, Lord of the twelve-thousand islands and Sultan of the Maldives* which came with the style *[Highness](/wiki/Highness "Highness")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The person traditionally deemed responsible for this conversion was a [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") Muslim visitor named Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) His venerated tomb now stands on the grounds of Medhu Ziyaaraiy, opposite[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the [Hukuru Mosque](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9_Friday_Mosque "Malé Friday Mosque") in the capital [Malé](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9 "Malé").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Built in 1656, this is the oldest mosque in Maldives.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

Scholars believe that the Islamization of the Maldives was a gradual process that likely took centuries, beginning before the official conversion of king Dhovemi and ending significantly after his conversion.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)

### Introduction of Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Introduction of Islam")]

**Maghrebi/Berber theory** Arab interest in the Maldives also was reflected in the residence there in the 1340s of [Ibn Battutah](/wiki/Ibn_Battutah "Ibn Battutah").[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) The well-known Moroccan traveller wrote how a Berber from North Morocco, one *Abu Barakat Yusuf the Berber*, was believed to have been responsible for spreading Islam in the islands, reportedly convincing the local king after having subdued Ranna Maari, a demon coming from the sea.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Even though this report has been contested in later sources, it does explain some crucial aspects of Maldivian culture. For instance, historically [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") has been the prime language of administration there, instead of the [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") languages used in the nearby Muslim states. Another link to [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") was the [Maliki](/wiki/Maliki "Maliki") school of jurisprudence, used throughout most of North Africa, which was the official one in the Maldives until the 17th century.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

#### Somali theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Somali theory")]

Some scholars have suggested the possibility of Ibn Battuta misreading Maldive texts, and having a bias or felt partial towards the North African Maghrebi/Berber narrative of this Shaykh. Instead of the East African origins account that was known as well at the time.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Even when Ibn Battuta visited an island of the Maldives, the governor of the island at that time was [Abd Aziz Al Mogadishawi](/wiki/Abd_al-Aziz_of_Mogadishu "Abd al-Aziz of Mogadishu"), a [Somali](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Also another prominent Shaykh on the island during Ibn Battuta's stay, was **Shaykh Najib al Habashi Al Salih**, another learned man from the Horn of Africa. His presence Indicating a strong Horn of African Islamic presence on the Island.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Scholars have spoken that **Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari** might have been a resident of [Berbera](/wiki/Berbera "Berbera"), a significant trading port on the north western coast of [Somaliland](/wiki/Somaliland "Somaliland").[[10]](#cite_note-10) *Barbara* or *Barbaroi* (Berbers), as the ancestors of the [Somalis](/wiki/Somali_people "Somali people") were referred to by medieval [Arab](/wiki/Arab_people "Arab people") and ancient [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") geographers, respectively.[[11]](#cite_note-Bagley22-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Mohamed_Diriye_Abdullahi_2001_p.132-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) This is also seen when Ibn Battuta visited [Mogadishu](/wiki/Mogadishu "Mogadishu"), he mentions that the Sultan at that time 'Abu Bakr ibn Shaikh Omar', was a Berber (Somali).

#### Persian theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Persian theory")]

Another interpretation, in the *Raadavalhi* and *Taarikh*,[[14]](#cite_note-Visweswaran2011-14)[[15]](#cite_note-sw-15) is that Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari was Abu al-Barakat Yusuf Shams ud-Din [at-Tabrizi](/wiki/Tabriz "Tabriz"), also locally known as Tabrīzugefānu. In the Arabic script the words al-Barbari and al-Tabrizi are very much alike, since, at the time, Arabic had several consonants that looked identical and could only be differentiated by overall context (this has since changed by addition of dots above or below letters to clarify pronunciation – For example, the letter "ب" in modern Arabic has a dot below, whereas the letter "ت" looks identical except there are two dots above it). "**ٮوسڡ الٮٮرٮرى**" could be read as "Yusuf at-Tabrizi" or "Yusuf al-Barbari".

## Islamic influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Islamic influence")]

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives"), and adherence to it is legally required for citizens by a revision of the constitution in 2008: Article 9, Section D and 10 states,

> *A non-Muslim may not become a citizen of the Maldives.*[[16]](#cite_note-ct2010-16)
> *The religion of the State of the Maldives is Islam. Islam shall be the one of the* [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] *basis of all the laws of the Maldives. No law contrary to any tenet of Islam shall be enacted in the Maldives.*[[17]](#cite_note-The_Constitution_of_Maldives-17)

The traditional [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") code of [sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") forms the Maldives' basic code of law, as interpreted to conform to local Maldivian conditions by the President, the attorney general, the Ministry of Home Affairs, and the Majlis.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Article 142 of the constitution states,

> *When deciding matters on which the Constitution or the law is silent, Judges must consider Islamic Shari’ah.*[[17]](#cite_note-The_Constitution_of_Maldives-17)

Proselytizing by non-Muslims in Maldives, including the public possession and distribution of non-Muslim religious materials (such as the Bible), is illegal. Public worship by adherents of religions other than Islam is forbidden.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

On the inhabited islands, the mosque forms the central place where Islam is practiced.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Because Friday is the most important day for Muslims to attend the mosque, shops and offices in towns and villages close around 11 a.m., reopening at 1 or 2 p.m., and the sermon begins by 12:35 p.m.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

The [prayer call](/wiki/Adhan "Adhan") is performed by the [*Mudhimu*](/wiki/Muezzin "Muezzin") (Muezzin).[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Most shops and offices close for fifteen minutes after each call.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) During the ninth Muslim month of [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan"), cafés and restaurants are closed during the day, and working hours are limited.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3)

## Mosques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Mosques")]

Most inhabited Maldivian islands have several [mosques](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque"); [Malé](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9 "Malé") has more than thirty.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) Most traditional mosques are whitewashed buildings constructed of coral stone with corrugated iron or thatched roofs.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) In addition to regular mosques are smaller, temporary mosques which accommodate fewer worshippers. These mosques are built in less time and labour in place of other mosques and buildings and will be demolished later on.

In Malé, the Islamic Centre and the Grand Friday Mosque, built in 1984 with funding from the [Persian Gulf states](/wiki/Persian_Gulf_states "Persian Gulf states"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), are imposing, elegant structures.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) The gold-colored dome of this mosque is the first structure sighted when approaching Malé.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) In mid-1991 Maldives had a total of 724 mosques and 266 women's mosques.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-3) However in recent years, mosques made especially for women have been demolished and reconstructed for other purposes. ## Radicalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Islam_in_the_Maldives&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Radicalism")]

*The Guardian* estimates that 50-100 fighters have joined [ISIS](/wiki/ISIS "ISIS") and [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") from the Maldives.[[19]](#cite_note-g150226-19) The Financial Times puts the number at 200.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

* [2007 Malé bombing](/wiki/2007_Mal%C3%A9_bombing "2007 Malé bombing"): On 29 September 2007 a homemade bomb went off in [Sultan Park](/w/index.php?title=Sultan%27s_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sultan's Park (page does not exist)") near the [Islamic Centre](/wiki/Islamic_Centre_(Maldives) "Islamic Centre (Maldives)") in the [Maldivian](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") capital [Malé](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9 "Malé"), injuring 12 foreign tourists. In December, three men were sentenced to 15 years in jail after they confessed to the bombing. Two of those imprisoned, Mohamed Sobah and Ahmed Amin - both Maldivian natives in their early twenties-had their sentences changed from incarceration to three-year suspended sentences under observation and were later set free in August 2010.[[21]](#cite_note-TheWeek2011-01-23-21)
* In February 2012 almost all the Maldives [National Museum](/wiki/National_Museum_(Maldives) "National Museum (Maldives)")'s pre-Islamic artifacts, dating back to before the 12th century, were destroyed during an attack: "Some of the pieces can be put together but mostly they are made of sandstone, coral and limestone, and they are reduced to powder." He said the museum had "nothing [left] to show" of the country's pre-Islamic history.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Among the damaged objects were a six-faced coral statue, an 18 in (46 cm) high bust of Buddha, as well as assorted limestone and coral statues[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2008 after the ratification of the new constitution, multi-party democracy was instated in the Maldives.

The Maldivian religious party named the [Adhaalath Party](/wiki/Adhaalath_Party "Adhaalath Party") was founded by the religious scholars and religious activists. Under the new freedom of speech and relaxed laws radicalism and different forms of religious factions rose in Maldives.

The religious extremism was controlled by the state, since in every presidency the coalition governments consisted with the Adhaalath Party's affiliation. The Islamic Ministry and major executive portfolio is represented by the Adaalath Party. In the Maldives, general clothing guidelines are observed as per democratic law instated and extremism is punishable by lengthy jail sentences.
